questions.question: Standard 7 of the Care Certificate is chiefly concerned with protecting which two things?
options: A person's diet and exercise
A person's finances and property
A person's medication and mobility
A person's privacy and dignity

questions.question: In this course, 'privacy' is described as having space that is your own and keeping control over which of the following?
options: Only your bedroom door being closed
The staff rota and shift patterns
Your body, your belongings and your personal information
Only the information held in your care records

questions.question: The Dignity in Care campaign and its 10 Dignity Do's are hosted by which organisation?
options: SCIE (the Social Care Institute for Excellence)
The Health and Safety Executive
NHS England
The Care Quality Commission (CQC)

questions.question: According to the material, the first of the 10 Dignity Do's is:
options: Always working at speed to save time
A zero tolerance of all forms of abuse
Keeping accurate written records
Offering people a choice of meals

questions.question: Article 8 of the Human Rights Act 1998 is described as a 'qualified right'. This means it:
options: Can occasionally be limited to keep someone or others safe, if the interference is proportionate and justified
Can be set aside whenever it is convenient for staff
Can never be limited under any circumstances
Only applies to people who lack capacity

questions.question: The 'need-to-know' principle asks you to consider which question before sharing a person's information?
options: Whether the person would ever find out
Whether this colleague actually needs this detail to do their job
Whether the information is interesting to colleagues
Whether it is the end of your shift

questions.question: Which framework requires care records to be kept accurately, securely and no longer than needed?
options: The Human Rights Act 1998
The UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018
The Equality Act 2010
CQC Regulation 10

questions.question: The statement 'confidentiality is not secrecy' is best explained as:
options: Sharing everything you know so colleagues are fully informed
Sharing what you must to keep someone safe and well, and no more
Never sharing any information about a person, whatever happens
Only ever sharing information with the person's family

questions.question: Which phrase does the course give as an example of childish or belittling language you should not use with an adult?
options: "Good morning"
"Good girl"
"Thank you for your patience"
"How would you like to do this?"

questions.question: Which Act is described as protecting things such as religion, race, sex, sexual orientation, age and disability from discrimination?
options: The Mental Capacity Act 2005
The Data Protection Act 2018
The Equality Act 2010
The Human Rights Act 1998

questions.question: In relation to the Mental Capacity Act, a decision that seems unwise:
options: Proves the person lacks capacity
Should always be reported as abuse
Is not, by itself, proof that someone lacks capacity
Must always be overruled by staff

questions.question: 'Active participation' means recognising each person as:
options: Someone who should be left to manage alone
An active partner in their own care and daily life
A task to be completed quickly
A passive recipient of care

questions.question: The principle sometimes summed up as 'nothing about me without me' is about:
options: Making decisions on the person's behalf to save time
Keeping care plans away from the person
Sharing information freely with everyone
Involving people in decisions about their own support

questions.question: Monitoring technology such as sensors or cameras should only be used when it is:
options: Requested by other residents
The cheapest option available
Genuinely in the person's interest and the least restrictive way to meet a need
Hidden so the person is not aware of it

questions.question: Confidentiality is absolute and must never be broken, even if someone is at risk of serious harm.
options: True
False

questions.question: A movement sensor that alerts staff to a fall may intrude far less on privacy than a camera watching a bedroom.
options: True
False

questions.question: Treating everyone exactly the same is the same as treating everyone fairly.
options: True
False

questions.question: Distress or agitation in a person living with dementia often signals an unmet need, such as discomfort, fear or a wish for privacy.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are ways to handle personal information the right way? (Select all that apply)
options: Pass on information to your manager if someone is at risk of harm
Seek consent where you can and explain who will see the information
Post updates about people you support on social media
Discuss the people you support in lifts and corridors
Lock away paper notes and log out of care systems

questions.question: Which of the following are principles for using monitoring technology (such as sensors or CCTV)? (Select all that apply)
options: Use the least restrictive, proportionate option to meet the need
Install hidden cameras without telling anyone
Use monitoring whenever it is convenient for staff
Be open with people and families about what is monitored and why
Gain the person's agreement where they can decide, or make and record a best-interests decision where they cannot

questions.question: Which of the following help you communicate with dignity when supporting a person living with dementia or a communication need? (Select all that apply)
options: Assume the person cannot follow you and talk over them
Assume understanding and speak to the person, not about them
Rush the person so you can finish the task quickly
Use a calm tone, eye contact, gestures, pictures or objects
Give the person longer to process what you say and to respond

questions.question: When supporting a person's dignity of risk, which of the following should you do? (Select all that apply)
options: Remove all risk to keep the person completely safe
Look for ways to make a chosen activity safer rather than simply forbidding it
Give clear information about the risks and benefits so the choice is informed
Forbid any choice that you personally think is unwise
Record the person's wishes and how risk has been discussed and managed

questions.question: The Equality Act 2010 protects certain ___ characteristics, such as religion, race and disability, from discrimination.
Active participation means treating the person as an active ___ in their own care, rather than a passive recipient of it.
Monitoring should only be used when it is the least ___ way to meet a person's need.
